text: During laparoscopy, the surgeon observes a torsion of the right ovarian cyst with gangrene. The right fallopian tube is visibly knotted with two to three turns around the ovary. The surgeon gently maneuvers to unrotate the torsion, revealing the right fallopian tube and the ureter with visible peristalsis.

After detorsion, the surgeon inspects the uterus and the left ovary, which appear normal. Using bipolar diathermy, the gangrenous right fallopian tube and ovary are dissected. The large gangrenous ovary and fimbrial cyst are manipulated primarily with the left hand, while the right side is maneuvered to divide the pedicle.
The surgeon carefully completes the division of the gangrenous fallopian tube and ovarian pedicle, ensuring no diathermy injury occurs to the surrounding structures.

The surgeon coagulates bleeding points and inspects the left ovary and uterus. A serous cyst is aspirated, removing 50 to 80 ml of fluid. The ovary and cyst are placed in an endobag for retrieval. Hemostasis is ensured, and fluid is suctioned out before the ovary and cyst are removed through the umbilical port using the endobag.
